data.title: Qualcomm downgraded to Neutral and placed on 'negative catalyst watch': JPMorgan
data.description:
Qualcomm (QCOM) was downgraded to Neutral from Overweight by J.P. Morgan due to a combination of rising competition in the data center space and downside risks to estimates in the near term.

The financial firm also lowered its price target to $140 from $185 and placed the stock on its "Negative Catalyst Watch" list.

"Qualcomm's long-term diversification strategy beyond smartphones has been part of our Overweight thesis on the shares through periods of macro and company-specific volatility in revenue drivers," said J.P. Morgan analysts, led by Samik Chatterjee, in an investor report. 

"In relation to the datacenter CPU and NPU opportunity within Datacenters, succesful execution could drive an inflection in non-smartphone revenues and deliver diversification ahead of its planned roadmap; but while the company is still working on its roadmap with customers in building pipeline of opportunities across both datacenter CPUs and NPUs, recent announcements from Arm (ARM) and Nvidia (NVDA) focused on Datacenter CPUs as well as chipsets to address inference use cases are increasing the competitive intensity and driving us to look more for execution datapoints from Qualcomm, which we expect will take time to emerge."

In the smartphone market, J.P. Morgan expects Qualcomm to face near-term pressure due to the memory supply shortage combined with its share loss from two of its premier customers, Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (SSNLF). 

In addition, the financial firm also pointed out macroeconomic concerns related to Qualcomm's Internet of Things and Automotive business segments.

J.P. Morgan reduced its revenue estimates for Qualcomm Technology Licensing. It reduced its fiscal year 2026 QTL revenue forecast to $5.3B from $5.5B. It also expects Qualcomm's wireless chipset technology, or QCT, business to decline by 22% in calendar year 2026 compared to the 17% consensus estimate. 

Qualcomm is slated to report its second quarter fiscal 2026 financial results post-market on April 29. A consensus estimate expects adjusted EPS of $2.56 and GAAP EPS of $1.88 on $10.57B in revenue.

During the second quarter of fiscal 2025, Qualcomm reported adjusted EPS of $2.85 and revenue of $10.84B.

data.title: Insteel outlines $20M 2026 capex as it expects gradual gross margin improvement in Q3
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insteel Industries (IIIN) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "Despite falling well short of our expected financial performance in Q2, we believe the upturn in business activity we reported previously is still intact." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • "We regret that we experienced both of these phenomena during Q2, but we're confident that short-term weather conditions and project delays neither create nor destroy demand and that postponed demand will be evident during the balance of fiscal 2026." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • "Our second quarter results were weaker than expected, reflecting the combined impact of winter weather disruptions, lower spreads and higher unit conversion costs." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "As we enter the third quarter, we expect several factors to support a recovery in gross margin." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "While we continue to believe that demand will be solid during 2026, we will reduce costs if this forecast fails to materialize." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Outlook

  • "Although we are still early in the third quarter, recent order activity has been solid, with April shipments trending above forecasted loans." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We expect these recent pricing actions, along with the additional price increase implemented in April to provide further benefit in the coming periods as they are more fully reflected in our realized pricing." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "At this point, however, we do not expect to be in a cost reduction mode driven by demand-related concerns." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • "As we closed out the second quarter, we remain encouraged by the demand trends we're seeing across our core end markets... At the same time, we recognize that these external factors could influence the pace of the activity in the near term." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Financial Results

  • "Net earnings for the quarter were $5.2 million or $0.27 per share compared with $10.2 million or $0.52 per diluted share in the same period last year." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "Shipments for the quarter declined 5.9% from the prior year but increased 6.9% sequentially from the first quarter." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "Gross profit declined $8 million year-over-year, $16.5 million, and gross margin narrowed to 9.6%." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "Operating cash flow provided $4.8 million in the current quarter compared with using $3.3 million of cash in the prior year period." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We incurred $4.4 million in capital expenditures in the quarter... and we remain committed to our full year target of $20 million." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • "We ended the quarter with $15.1 million of cash on hand and no borrowings outstanding on our $100 million revolving credit facility, providing us ample liquidity and financial flexibility going forward." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Q&A

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked how much delayed projects weighed on shipments and what drove delays; President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz answered, "this is a delay of business. It's not a cancellation" and added, "we should begin shipping it in the current quarter... this project will go through our fiscal year and into 2027." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Julio Romero, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked whether April shipment strength reflected delayed projects or weather catch-up; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "I don't think any of it is related to project delays" and added, "the current performance... is pretty solid relative to our expectations and our prices are coming up as we expected them to." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about freight cost pass-through and recovery; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we debated surcharges or we debated price increases, and we've elected just to increase our prices" and added, "I wouldn't say we recovered them prospectively." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked magnitude and cadence of price increases; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we've implemented 3 since the first of the year" and described a lag effect: "we are honoring the commitments that we've made" and "it would be the next... orders that are affected by price increases." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked about tariff clarification and potential refunds; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said Section 232 clarifications "don't have a whole lot of impact on us" and added, "we certainly will not be booking any kinds of receivables for tariff collections because I think it's highly improbable that it will happen in any simplistic kind of way." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

  • Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates: Asked whether data center builds are prone to being pushed out; President, CEO & Chairman Waltz said, "we don't think that the data center phenomenon goes away in 2026 or '27" and added, "I think you have 5 solid years of data center activity." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone appeared neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on shipment deferrals, freight recovery, tariff mechanics, and the risk of data center timing being "pushed to the right." (Tyson Bauer, Kansas City Capital Associates)

  • Management tone in prepared remarks was slightly negative on Q2 performance but more confident about recovery, including "we're confident" on postponed demand and "we expect" gross margin recovery drivers; in Q&A, management was more matter-of-fact and occasionally cautionary, including "I wouldn't say we recovered them prospectively" and "we certainly will not be booking any kinds of receivables." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz; VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s more upbeat framing ("we delivered a strong start to the year" and "we are encouraged"), the current quarter emphasized disruption and lag effects while keeping a recovery narrative, including "we expect several factors to support a recovery in gross margin." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 emphasized momentum and acquisitions, including "The upturn in business activity... continued" and "our fiscal 2025 acquisitions continue to perform well," while Q2 emphasized underperformance and temporary disruption: "we were affected during Q2 by weather-related and non-weather-related circumstances." (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • Q1 described pricing as stable sequentially with "we did not take additional pricing actions during the current period" and then a January increase; Q2 emphasized repeated actions and lag, including "we've implemented 3 since the first of the year" and an April increase expected to flow through realized pricing. (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz; VP, CFO & Treasurer Jafroodi)

  • Both quarters highlighted wire rod tightness and offshore sourcing, but Q2 updated working capital pressure: "Net working capital rose approximately $45 million over the last 12 months" and reiterated continued importing "until such time as domestic availability improves." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Winter weather is a fact of life in our business" and Q2 experienced "severe and prolonged" conditions that disrupted construction activity and operating schedules. (President, CEO & Chairman H.O. Waltz)

  • "The broader macroeconomic backdrop continues to evolve, including the risk of renewed inflation, uncertainty around the timing of interest rate cuts, potential changes in tariff policy and the geopolitical developments affecting energy and shipping costs." (VP, CFO & Treasurer Scot Jafroodi)

  • "The economics of offshore transactions... require the purchase of large quantities with the resulting impact on inventories and net working capital requirements" as the company imports wire rod amid reduced domestic capacity; management said it has "some options to mitigate this adverse impact." (President, CEO & Chairman Waltz)

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a weather- and timing-impacted quarter with weaker spreads and higher conversion costs, while maintaining that demand was delayed rather than lost. Entering Q3, management pointed to improving seasonal demand, traction from multiple price increases (including one in April), more favorable raw material carrying values, and higher operating rates as the basis for a gradual margin recovery, while highlighting ongoing external risks spanning tariffs, freight, and broader macro uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta raises price of Quest VR headsets to compensate for higher chip costs
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) is raising the cost of its Quest virtual reality headsets to compensate for the increased cost of memory chips.

“We’re making this change because the cost of building high-performance VR hardware has risen significantly,” the company said on its website. “The global surge in the price of critical components—specifically memory chips—is impacting almost every category of consumer electronics, including VR.”

Beginning April 19, the price of the Meta Quest 3S (128 GB) and Meta Quest 3S (256 GB) will go up $50 to $349.99 and $449.99, respectively, and the cost of the Meta Quest 3 (256 GB) will increase by $100 to $599.99.   

Updated pricing will also apply to refurbished units. 

While Meta (META) reassured its customers that it remains committed to investing in VR and “leading the category,” the company recently cut 10% of its Reality Labs staff amid a pivot from virtual reality to generative artificial intelligence.

data.title: Musk's xAI to rent computing power to Cursor: report
data.description:
Amid the insatiable demand for computing power, xAI (X.AI) appears likely to rent some of its capacity to artificial intelligence startup Cursor, Business Insider reported. 

Cursor is likely to train its latest AI model, known as Composer 2.5, on “thousands” of GPUs that xAI has obtained from suppliers, such as Nvidia (NVDA), the news outlet reported. The move could position xAI, which is now a part of SpaceX (SPACE), as a potential alternative to the hyperscalers, such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), and the neo-clouds, such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS), the news outlet added. It could also boost xAI's revenue streams as SpaceX reportedly prepares to go public amid high investor expectations.

Elon Musk, who co-founded xAI a few years ago, apologized in March for not building xAI “right” and said it was being rebuilt.

Cursor, which reportedly hit $2B in annual recurring revenue last month, is in talks to raise additional funding that would value the AI coding company at $50B.

Neither xAI nor Cursor immediately responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Sustained oil prices essential before markets can see fundamental risk-taking – analyst
data.description:
Sustained lower oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) are essential before the market can see a return of fundamental risk-taking, according to Alli McCartney, managing director at UBS Alignment Partners.

While recent rallies have pushed markets near all-time highs, McCartney cautioned that the current momentum reflects technical factors rather than renewed investor confidence.

In an interview with CNBC, McCartney explained that the recent recovery in beaten-down sectors like software (XSW), (IGV) and alternative asset managers stems from short covering rather than fresh investment.

“What you are seeing in terms of the technicals of the market or what’s actually happening at a micro level is not exactly renewed risk-taking yet, but rather short covering,” she said.

Despite the technical nature of the rally, McCartney acknowledged bullish tailwinds from an impressive earnings season showing 17% growth. She noted the breadth is particularly encouraging, with average companies up eight to 10% rather than gains concentrated in just a few top performers.

“That is a very different level of breadth than we’ve seen in past earning seasons,” she observed.

However, oil remains the dominant bearish concern. “The biggest bearish story out there is still the price of oil and the long-term macro effects, not only in the U.S., but especially outside of the U.S.,” McCartney said.

She added that while markets may have overreacted to the downside, the rapid swing back upward could also prove excessive.

On the positive side, McCartney pointed to ISM manufacturing entering expansionary territory for the first time in five years and a labor market that is “precarious but potentially balanced.”

Still, she emphasized that April and May data will be critical in revealing the true impact of elevated fuel costs on consumers.

The real-world burden of high gas prices (XB1:COM) remains a persistent concern. McCartney recounted paying $8 a gallon during a recent trip to California, underscoring the strain on American consumers. “That doesn’t go away,” she said.

data.title: Shroom stocks rise as Trump is said to sign executive order on psychedelic drug
data.description:
• Psychedelic stocks traded higher on Thursday after CBS News reported that President Donald Trump is expected to issue an executive order that would signal his administration’s intention to increase research into ibogaine, a traditional psychedelic medicine in Africa.
  • Notable gainers include Atai Life Sciences (ATAI), COMPASS Pathways (CMPS), GH Research (GHRS), Mind Medicine (MNMD), Cybin (HELP), and Bright Minds Biosciences (DRUG).
  • The White House is currently drafting the order, which, according to sources, aims to pave the way for federal funding for further research on ibogaine’s effectiveness against post-traumatic stress disorder and traumatic brain injuries, especially among veterans.
  • Trump is expected to sign the executive action as early as this week, two of the sources said. However, there are no plans to reclassify ibogaine for medical use—the drug will remain under Schedule I of the Controlled Substances Act.

data.title: Industrials trade at record premium as defense, AI, oil themes collide - BofA
data.description:
Industrial stocks (XLI) have surged to their most expensive valuations relative to the S&P 500 (SPY) (VOO) in nearly four decades, Bank of America Securities said in a Thursday note, as the sector attracts heavy investor interest across multiple themes.

The firm said industrials (XLI) now trade at peak relative price-to-forward earnings, with buyside exposure at record levels. Investors are treating the sector as a bundled bet on defense spending, artificial intelligence-linked capital investment and energy sensitivity.

BofA noted that many non-energy companies with high oil betas sit inside industrials, allowing the sector to function as “closet energy” for portfolios facing constraints around owning fossil fuel producers directly.

That dynamic has helped industrials benefit even as hedge funds' energy (XLE) allocations remain structurally light, the note said.

Below is a list of the top 10 highest-ranked industrial stocks according to the Seeking Alpha Quant system (ratings are given on a scale from 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered bullish and any rating of 2.5 or below considered bearish):

  • Babcock & Wilcox Enterprises, Inc. (BW); Quant rating: 4.99; Industry: Heavy Electrical Equipment; Market cap: $2.48B

  • Commercial Vehicle Group, Inc. (CVGI), 4.98, Construction Machinery & Heavy Transportation Equipment, $134.86M

  • Tigo Energy, Inc. (TYGO), 4.95, Electrical Components and Equipment, $303.44M

  • Seanergy Maritime Holdings Corp. (SHIP), 4.93, Marine Transportation, $315.71M

  • Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD), 4.91, Building Products, $12.56B

  • FedEx Corporation (FDX), 4.91, Air Freight and Logistics, $87.07B

  • Diana Shipping Inc. (DSX), 4.90, Marine Transportation, $279.28M

  • ATI Inc. (ATI), 4.85, Aerospace and Defense, $21.40B

  • Deluxe Corporation (DLX), 4.84, Commercial Printing, $1.30B

  • MYR Group Inc. (MYRG), 4.80, Construction and Engineering, $4.98B

data.title: NextNav surges after Oppenheimer upgrade
data.description:
NextNav (NN) shares gained over 20% during afternoon trading on Thursday after Oppenheimer upgraded the stock to Outperform from Perform, with a price target of $25.

The brokerage firm believes that the FCC Notice of Proposed Rule Making (NPRM) approval of NN's re-banding is imminent.

Oppenheimer analyst Timothy Horan highlighted that the company would receive an additional 1 MHz of spectrum, giving it a contiguous 15 MHz (5.1B MHz-POPs) in the lower 900 MHz band.

“The spectrum could be worth substantially more than  $0.80, depending on FCC restrictions on power and coverage, which will be disclosed in the NPRM, similar recent sales have been above $2 per MHz-POP.”

The brokerage firm also sees several strategic acquirers across wireless/satellite, with AT&T the most logical fit, but SpaceX and Verizon Communications are also credible buyers.

NextNav shares have gained nearly 24% so far this year, outperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has gained 2.6% during the same period.

data.title: Health Care ETFs take notice as Abbott Laboratories slides on earnings
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and exchange traded funds with significant exposure to the healthcare company drew investor attention Thursday after the stock declined 4.5% following its latest earnings release.

Despite posting first-quarter revenue above expectations—supported by solid performance in its MedTech segment—the company revised its full-year 2026 earnings outlook lower. Abbott now projects adjusted earnings per share between $5.38 and $5.58, reflecting an estimated $0.20 dilution tied to its acquisition of Exact Sciences. 

The updated guidance appeared to outweigh the otherwise strong quarterly results, prompting a negative market reaction.

Abbott’s broad presence across the ETF landscape adds another layer of market impact. The company is currently held in 365 exchange traded funds, which collectively own more than 306M shares. As a result, price movements in Abbott shares may have ripple effects across a range of healthcare focused and diversified ETFs with sizable allocations to the stock. 

Listed below are the 10 funds with the largest allocations towards ABT: 

  1. iShares U.S. Medical Devices ETF (IHI), 17.26% allocation. 
  2. Diamond Hill Large Cap Concentrated ETF (DHLX), 5.66% allocation. 
  3. Invesco Pharmaceuticals ETF (PJP), 5.05% allocation. 
  4. T. Rowe Price Health Care ETF (TMED), 4.09% allocation.
  5. YieldMax U.S. Stocks Target Double Distribution ETF (DDDD), 3.80% allocation.
  6. Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), 3.79% allocation.
  7. AdvisorShares Focused Equity ETF (CWS), 3.61% allocation. 
  8. Castellan Targeted Income ETF (CTIF), 3.48% allocation.
  9. Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV), 3.45% allocation. 
  10. iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (IYH), 3.36% allocation. 

Other healthcare ETFs for further analysis: (VHT), (IXJ), (FHLC), and (FXH).

data.title: Mortgage rates down to four-week low
data.description:
Long-term mortgage rates declined to a four-week low, according to the latest Freddie Mac (FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.30% as of April 16, down from 6.37% last week and 6.83% a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.65%, down from 5.74% last week and 6.03% in the year-ago week.

"Compared to one year ago when rates were at 6.83%, this is a meaningful improvement for homebuyers during what is typically the busy spring homebuying season," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.
